learn.title: Learning Academy
learn.subtitle: Master the art of investing from basics to advanced strategies
learn.correctAnswer: Excellent! You've grasped the core concept.

learn.quote: The individual investor should act consistently as an investor and not as a speculator.

m1.title: Market Foundations
m1.desc: Understand how the stock market works, primary vs secondary markets, and what it means to own a share.
m1.theory: The stock market is where shares of publicly held companies are issued and traded. It consists of the Primary Market (IPOs) where companies raise capital, and the Secondary Market (Exchanges) where investors trade with each other. Buying a stock means you own a fractional interest in that company.

quiz.q1: Where do investors trade existing shares with each other?

m1.simulation: Goal: Explore the Market and identify a high-volume stock to understand supply and demand.

m2.title: The Intelligent Investor
m2.desc: Learn the principles of value investing from Benjamin Graham: Margin of Safety and Mr. Market.
m2.theory: The Intelligent Investor focuses on 'Value Investing'. The 'Margin of Safety' is buying stocks at a discount to their intrinsic value. 'Mr. Market' is an allegory for the emotional swings of the market; an intelligent investor remains calm and exploits these swings rather than participating in them.

quiz.q1: The practice of buying a stock significantly below its intrinsic value is called:

m2.simulation: Goal: Find a stable stock and buy $500 worth of shares to practice long-term holding.

m3.title: Portfolio Strategy
m3.desc: Master diversification and understand your risk tolerance.
m3.theory: Diversification is the practice of spreading your investments across various industries to reduce risk. Graham distinguishes between 'Defensive' investors (passive) and 'Enterprising' investors (active). Your goal is to build a portfolio that matches your risk profile.

quiz.q1: What is the main benefit of diversification?

m3.simulation: Goal: Buy stocks from two different sectors (e.g., Tech and Retail) to diversify your risk.

m4.title: Brokers in Colombia
m4.desc: Learn all the local stock brokers to trade on the BVC and find the best one for you.
m4.theory:
A broker or stock broker is an entity authorized by the Colombian Financial Superintendency to act as an intermediary in the buying and selling of securities on the Bogotá Stock Exchange (BVC). Brokers are essential because individual investors cannot trade directly on the exchange: they need an authorized intermediary.

TYPES OF BROKERS IN COLOMBIA

1. INTEGRAL BROKERS: The most common and offer complete custody, trading, and advisory services. Examples: Serfinco, Correval, Scotia Valores.

2. AUTHORIZED BROKERS: Can trade on the BVC but offer more limited services. Examples: Alianza Valores, Casa de Bolsa.

ALL REGISTERED BROKERS IN COLOMBIA:

• SERFINCO: One of the most popular platforms, known for its user-friendly platform and competitive commissions. Founded in 1962.

• CORREVAL: Part of the Bolívar Group, offers comprehensive services and has national presence with multiple offices.

• SCOTIA VALORES COLOMBIA: Part of Scotiabank, offers services for all investor levels.

• ALIANZA VALORES: Broker focused on personalized advisory.
• CASA DE BOLSA: Subsidiary of Grupo Aval, offers custody and trading services.

• BVC (Bolsa de Valores de Colombia): In addition to being the exchange, it offers brokerage services directly.

• CRED IQ: Broker specializing in institutional investors and large fortunes.

• GLOBAL BANK: Bank with brokerage division.

• BANCO POPULAR: Has integrated brokerage division.

• LTG TRUST: Specializing in trusts and wealth management.
• HELM BANK: Brokerage services for high-net-worth clients.


HOW TO OPEN AN ACCOUNT:


1. Choose a broker that fits your profile
2. Gather documents: ID, income certificate, bank statements
3. Complete account opening form
4. Deposit minimum amount (varies by broker, from $500,000 COP)
5. Wait for validation and activation (1-5 business days)

TYPICAL COMMISSIONS:

• Trading commission: 0.1% - 0.5% of transaction value
• Custody: $0 - $25,000 COP monthly
• Withdrawals: $0 - $15,000 COP
• Some platforms offer flat packages from $25,000 COP/month

FUN FACTS:

• The Bogotá Stock Exchange (BVC) was founded in 2001, merging the stock exchanges of Bogotá, Medellín and Barranquilla.

• The first million operations in the BVC was reached in just 3 years.

• Colombia is the third largest stock exchange in Latin America after Brazil and Mexico.

• Many Colombian brokers offer accounts with as little as $500,000 COP (~$100 USD) to start.


• Trading hours at the BVC are from 9:00 AM to 4:00 PM Colombia time.

quiz.q1: What entity authorizes brokers to operate in Colombia?

m4.simulation: Goal: Explore the platforms of different brokers and compare their commissions and services.

m5.title: Cryptocurrencies
m5.desc: Understand the world of cryptocurrencies, blockchain, and how they differ from traditional stocks.
m5.theory:
Cryptocurrencies are decentralized digital currencies that use cryptography to secure transactions and control the creation of new units. Unlike stocks, they are not backed by a company or grant ownership rights.

HOW BLOCKCHAIN WORKS

Blockchain is a distributed ledger technology that records transactions securely and transparently. Each 'block' of transactions connects to the previous one, forming an immutable chain.

• Decentralization: No central authority controls the network
• Immutability: Once a transaction is recorded, it cannot be modified
• Transparency: Anyone can verify transactions on the public chain

THE TWO MAIN CRYPTOCURRENCIES:

BITCOIN (BTC):
• Launched in 2009 by an anonymous person 'Satoshi Nakamoto'
• The first million bitcoins were mined in the early days
• Original price in 2010: ~$0.001 USD (10,000 BTC = 1 pizza)
• Today worth thousands of dollars per bitcoin
• Maximum of 21 million bitcoins (hardcoded limit)
• Uses proof of work (PoW)

ETHEREUM (ETH):
• Launched in 2015 by Vitalik Buterin
• Enables smart contracts and decentralized applications (dApps)
• No maximum supply limit
• Is migrating to proof of stake (PoS)

TYPES OF WALLETS:

• HOT WALLETS: Connected to the internet, more convenient but less secure. Examples: Binance, Coinbase, Exodus.

• COLD WALLETS: Offline, more secure. Examples: Ledger, Trezor, paper wallets.


DIFFERENCES WITH STOCKS:

| Aspect | Stocks | Cryptocurrencies |
|--------|--------|-------------------|
| Regulation | YES (SFC Colombia) | Partial/Minimal |
| Rights | Ownership, voting | None (most) |
| Volatility | Moderate | Extreme |
| Hours | Exchange hours only | 24/7 |
| Liquidity | High (popular stocks) | Variable |

REGULATION IN COLOMBIA:

• The DIAN has established that cryptocurrencies are not legal tender in Colombia.
• Transactions with cryptocurrencies may have tax implications.
• There is currently no clear regulatory framework for cryptocurrency exchanges.

FUN FACTS:

• The first real purchase with Bitcoin was in 2010: 10,000 BTC for 2 pizzas = ~$25 USD
• At its peak, Bitcoin reached almost $69,000 USD (November 2021)
• There are more than 10,000 different cryptocurrencies, but most have little or no value
• The name 'Bitcoin' first appears in a 1998 article by Wei Dai, 11 years before its creation
• There is a wallet with 10,000 BTC that has never moved since 2010, now worth hundreds of millions

RISKS TO CONSIDER:

• Extreme volatility: Prices can move 20%+ in a single day
• No protection: No guarantee fund like bank deposits
• Scams: Hackers, phishing, rug pulls (developers abandon project)
• Regulatory uncertainty: Can change at any time

quiz.q1: What was the first real thing bought with Bitcoin?

m5.simulation: Goal: Explore the Bitcoin price chart and observe its historical volatility.

m6.title: International Finance
m6.desc: Discover global markets, ADRs, Forex, and how to invest around the world.
m6.theory:
International finance encompasses all instruments, markets, and participants operating beyond a country's borders. Investing globally allows for risk diversification and access to unique opportunities.

THE MAIN GLOBAL MARKETS:

1. NYSE (NEW YORK): The largest stock exchange in the world by market cap.
• More than 2,400 listed companies
• Companies like Apple, Microsoft, Berkshire Hathaway
• Hours: 9:30 AM - 4:00 PM EST

2. NASDAQ (NEW YORK): Center of technology and innovation.
• Home of Apple, Amazon, Google, Tesla
• More than 3,000 companies
• Hours: 9:30 AM - 4:00 PM EST

3. LONDON STOCK EXCHANGE (LSE): The sixth largest in the world.
• Historically one of the oldest (1688)
• Global companies like BP, HSBC, AstraZeneca
• Hours: 8:00 AM - 4:30 PM GMT

4. TOKYO STOCK EXCHANGE (JPX): Main market in Asia.
• More than 3,800 companies
• Companies like Toyota, Sony, SoftBank
• Hours: 9:00 AM - 3:00 PM JST (with lunch break)

5. HONG KONG STOCK EXCHANGE (HKEX): Gateway to China.
• Alibaba, Tencent, Meituan
• Hours: 9:30 AM - 4:00 PM HKT

ADRs (AMERICAN DEPOSITARY RECEIPTS):

ADRs are certificates representing shares of foreign companies traded on US exchanges. For Colombians, investing in ADRs is the easiest way to access companies like Google, Apple, or Amazon.


• ADRs trade in US dollars
• Listed on NYSE or NASDAQ
• Each ADR may represent 1, 2, 4 or more shares of the original company
• Popular examples: AAPL (Apple), GOOGL (Google), MSFT (Microsoft), AMZN (Amazon), TSLA (Tesla)


FOREX (FOREIGN EXCHANGE MARKET):


The foreign exchange market is the largest in the world:

• Daily volume: +$7.5 trillion dollars daily
• Operates 24 hours, 5 days a week
• Major pairs: EUR/USD, GBP/USD, USD/JPY
• Participants: Central banks, commercial banks, corporations, speculators

• FUN FACT: The EUR/USD pair is the most traded, representing ~85% of total volume

EXCHANGE RATES AND COLOMBIA:

• The Colombian peso (COP) is relatively stable compared to other currencies in the region
• The US dollar (USD) is the most traded currency against the COP
• Factors affecting the exchange rate: oil prices, investment flows, Banco de la República policies

GLOBAL DIVERSIFICATION:

Investing globally offers benefits:

• Reduced country risk
• Access to sectors not available locally (e.g., Chinese tech)
• Greater market depth
• Growth opportunities in emerging markets

FUN FACTS:


• The world's oldest stock market is the Amsterdam Stock Exchange, founded in 1602 by the Dutch East India Company.
• The NYSE was created in 1817, originally was a group of brokers under a tree.
• When Apple announced a 4:1 split in 2020, the price dropped from ~$500 to ~$125, but the company's total value didn't change.

• In 1987, 'Black Monday' the Dow Jones fell 22.6% in a single day, the worst collapse in history.
• The biggest single-day move in history: Bitcoin fell 50% in hours in March 2020 during COVID.
• Walmart was the world's largest company by revenue until 2023.
• The combined market cap of Apple, Microsoft, and Alphabet exceeds the GDP of most countries.
• The Forex market operates 24 hours because when New York closes, Tokyo opens, and when Tokyo closes, London opens.
• The 'Big Bang' in 1986 in London radically changed stock trading.

quiz.q1: What is the largest currency market in the world by daily volume?

m6.simulation: Goal: Explore an ADR (like AAPL or GOOGL) and understand how to invest in global companies.
